class_schedule.topic: What Is a Game? Definitions and Core Elements
learning_objectives: Define what constitutes a game versus other forms of interactive media
Trace the evolution of games from ancient board games through modern digital experiences
Identify the core elements present in all games: rules, goals, feedback, voluntary participation
Distinguish the role of the game designer from other development disciplines
readings: The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 1-2
Rules of Play by Salen & Zimmerman, Ch. 1
Homo Ludens by Johan Huizinga, Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction:
Welcome to Introduction to Game Design. This course provides the foundational vocabulary, analytical frameworks, and practical skills every game designer needs. Over the next fifteen weeks you will learn how games are conceived, prototyped, tested, and refined. By the end of the semester you will have designed, playtested, and documented original game concepts from scratch.

Before we can design games, we need to agree on what a game actually is. That turns out to be a surprisingly contentious question. Philosophers, designers, and scholars have proposed dozens of definitions, each emphasizing different aspects of the experience. Today we will survey the most influential definitions and extract the elements they share.

The history of games stretches back at least five thousand years. The Egyptian game of Senet, the Chinese game of Go, and the Indian game of Chaturanga all predate written history in some form. Understanding where games come from helps us see that the design problems we face today, creating fair rules, balancing skill and chance, keeping players engaged, are as old as civilization itself. Digital technology has expanded the medium enormously, but the underlying design challenges remain remarkably consistent.
key_concepts: Huizinga's 'magic circle': the boundary between ordinary life and the play space where special rules apply
Caillois' four categories of play: Agon (competition), Alea (chance), Mimicry (make-believe), Ilinx (vertigo/sensation)
Costikyan's definition: a game is a form of art in which participants make decisions to manage resources through game tokens in pursuit of a goal
Schell's definition: a game is a problem-solving activity approached with a playful attitude
Salen & Zimmerman: a game is a system in which players engage in an artificial conflict, defined by rules, that results in a quantifiable outcome
Core elements shared by all definitions: rules that constrain behavior, goals that direct effort, feedback that communicates progress, voluntary participation that maintains engagement
Games as formal systems: interlocking parts where changing one element affects every other element
The evolution timeline: Senet (3100 BCE) to Go to Chess to card games to Monopoly to Pong to World of Warcraft to Fortnite
The designer's role versus programmer, artist, producer, and QA roles in modern development
Games versus toys, puzzles, and simulations: where the boundaries blur and why it matters for design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative structural analysis of three games spanning different eras (Checkers, Tetris, Minecraft) to identify which core elements each shares and where they diverge.
setup_requirements: Notebook or digital document for your design journal (you will maintain this all semester)
Access to at least two different games (any platform) for hands-on analysis

review.value: A system in which players engage in an artificial conflict, defined by rules, that results in a quantifiable outcome. Multiple valid definitions exist but all share rules, goals, feedback, and voluntary participation.
Huizinga's concept of a bounded space where the rules of the game supersede the rules of ordinary life, creating a shared social contract among players.
Rules constrain what players can do, goals direct what players want to do, feedback tells players how they are doing, and voluntary participation ensures players choose to be there.
Games are composed of interlocking parts. Changing one rule, one resource, or one goal can cascade through the entire system, which is why design requires systemic thinking.

class_schedule.topic: History of Games: From Tabletop to Digital
learning_objectives: Identify key milestones in game history and explain their design significance
Analyze how technological change has expanded the possibility space for game designers
Connect historical design innovations to conventions still used in modern games
readings: A History of Video Games in 64 Objects by World Video Game Hall of Fame, selected entries
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 3
Extra Credits: 'The History of Fun' video series (Episodes 1-3)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Understanding the history of games is not an academic exercise in nostalgia. Every convention you encounter in modern game design, health bars, save systems, difficulty settings, was invented by someone solving a specific problem at a specific moment in time. When you know why a convention exists, you can decide intelligently whether to follow it, modify it, or discard it entirely.

Today we trace the arc from ancient tabletop games through the arcade era, the console revolution, the rise of PC gaming, the mobile explosion, and the current landscape of free-to-play and live-service models. At each stage, we will ask the same question: what new design possibilities did this technology unlock, and how did designers exploit them?

Pay particular attention to how constraints drove creativity. The Atari 2600 had 128 bytes of RAM. Game Boy had a 160x144 pixel monochrome screen. These limitations forced designers to communicate with extreme economy, producing some of the most elegant designs in the medium's history. Understanding constraints as creative fuel is one of the most important lessons in this course.
key_concepts: Ancient games: Senet, Go, Chess, Mancala, and the design principles they established (territorial control, perfect information, resource management)
The arcade era: coin-operated design incentivizing short sessions, high difficulty, and the 'just one more quarter' loop
The home console revolution: longer sessions, save systems, narrative arcs, and the shift from score-chasing to goal-completion
The PC as design laboratory: modding communities, real-time strategy, first-person shooters, and MMORPGs
Handheld and mobile gaming: touch interfaces, casual audiences, session-length constraints, and free-to-play monetization
The indie renaissance: small teams creating innovative designs outside publisher constraints (Braid, Minecraft, Undertale)
Live-service and games-as-platform: Fortnite, Destiny, and the design challenges of games that never end
How technological constraints shaped iconic designs: Pac-Man's maze AI, Doom's BSP rendering, Pokemon's link cable trading
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on play session with three games from different eras (a classic board game, an Atari 2600 ROM via emulator, a modern indie title) followed by group discussion of how design priorities shifted across eras.
setup_requirements: Access to a classic board game (Chess, Checkers, or Go)
Web browser for Atari emulator (archive.org)
Design journal

review.value: Severe technological limitations often produce the most elegant designs because designers must communicate maximum meaning with minimum resources.
Coin-operated games were designed for short, intense sessions with high difficulty to maximize revenue per play, establishing conventions like lives, continues, and score displays.
Digital distribution and accessible tools enabled small teams to take creative risks that large publishers would not fund, producing some of the most innovative designs of the 21st century.
Games designed to run indefinitely face unique challenges: content treadmills, power creep, community management, and balancing new player onboarding with veteran retention.

class_schedule.topic: The MDA Framework: Mechanics, Dynamics, Aesthetics
learning_objectives: Explain the MDA framework and its three interconnected layers
Analyze existing games by decomposing them into mechanics, dynamics, and aesthetics
Distinguish the designer's perspective (mechanics-first) from the player's perspective (aesthetics-first)
Apply MDA analysis to identify why a specific design succeeds or fails
readings: MDA: A Formal Approach to Game Design and Game Research by Hunicke, LeBlanc, Zubek (original paper)
A Theory of Fun for Game Design by Raph Koster, Ch. 1-3
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction:
Designing a good game requires structured thinking. Without a framework, design conversations devolve into subjective assertions like 'this feels fun' or 'that part is boring.' The MDA framework gives us a shared vocabulary for reasoning about why games work or fail, separating the discussion into three distinct layers.

Mechanics are the rules and systems that the designer directly controls: how many hit points a character has, how fast gravity pulls, what cards are in the deck. Dynamics are the runtime behaviors that emerge when players interact with those mechanics: an arms race between two factions, a tense bluff in poker, a desperate last-stand defense. Aesthetics are the emotional responses the player experiences: the thrill of narrowly winning, the satisfaction of solving a puzzle, the wonder of discovering a hidden area.

The critical insight of MDA is that designers and players approach the game from opposite directions. The designer starts with mechanics and hopes they produce the right aesthetics. The player starts with the emotional experience and has no idea (or interest in) the underlying mechanics. When a game fails, the disconnect between intended and actual aesthetics is almost always traceable to a mechanical cause. MDA gives us the language to diagnose that disconnect precisely.
key_concepts: Mechanics: the formal rules, algorithms, and data structures that compose the game system as designed by the developer
Dynamics: the runtime behavior that emerges when players interact with mechanics, often producing patterns the designer did not explicitly author
Aesthetics: the emotional responses evoked in the player, categorized into eight types by LeBlanc
The eight aesthetics: Sensation, Fantasy, Narrative, Challenge, Fellowship, Discovery, Expression, Submission (also called Abnegation)
Designer vs. player perspective: designers work Mechanics to Dynamics to Aesthetics; players experience Aesthetics to Dynamics to Mechanics
Using MDA for diagnosis: when players report the game 'feels unfair,' trace the aesthetic (frustration) back through dynamics (runaway leader) to mechanics (unchecked positive feedback loop)
Iteration as the heart of design: the MDA loop of design, prototype, playtest, analyze, revise
Constraints as creative fuel: limiting mechanics often sharpens dynamics and clarifies aesthetics
Comparing MDA analyses: how the same mechanic (limited inventory space) produces different aesthetics in survival horror versus cozy farming simulation
Limitations of MDA: the framework describes but does not prescribe; it tells you what happened, not what to build next
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full MDA deconstruction of Super Mario Bros. and Dark Souls side by side, showing how both use jumping and combat mechanics but produce radically different aesthetic experiences due to differences in dynamics like punishment severity and checkpoint spacing.
setup_requirements: Printed MDA reference sheet (provided)
Design journal for in-class analysis exercise

review.value: Mechanics are the rules, Dynamics are the runtime behavior that emerges from players interacting with those rules, Aesthetics are the emotional responses players experience.

Sensation (sensory pleasure), Fantasy (make-believe), Narrative (drama), Challenge (obstacle course), Fellowship (social connection), Discovery (exploration), Expression (self-discovery), Submission (game as pastime).
Designers work from mechanics outward toward aesthetics; players experience aesthetics first and may never understand the mechanics that produce them.
When a playtest reveals an unintended aesthetic (boredom, frustration, confusion), trace backward through dynamics to find the mechanical root cause.

learning_objectives: Identify intrinsic and extrinsic motivators in games and explain when each is effective
Apply Bartle's player taxonomy and its extensions to design decisions
Explain Csikszentmihalyi's flow theory and its application to game difficulty design
Recognize the overjustification effect and its implications for reward design
readings: The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 8-9
Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience by Csikszentmihalyi, Ch. 4
Designing Virtual Worlds by Richard Bartle, Ch. 3 (player types)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Understanding why people play games is as important as understanding how games work mechanically. Every design decision you make, from reward timing to difficulty scaling to social features, is an implicit bet about what motivates your players. Getting that bet wrong means players leave.

Self-Determination Theory (SDT), developed by Deci and Ryan, identifies three core psychological needs that drive intrinsic motivation: autonomy (the feeling of choice and control), competence (the feeling of mastery and growth), and relatedness (the feeling of connection with others). Games that satisfy all three needs tend to produce deep, lasting engagement. Games that rely on extrinsic motivators alone, points, badges, leaderboards, often see engagement drop sharply once the rewards stop escalating.

Csikszentmihalyi's concept of flow describes a mental state of complete absorption where challenge and skill are perfectly balanced. When a game is too easy, players get bored. When it is too hard, they get anxious. The narrow channel between boredom and anxiety is the flow zone, and keeping players in it is one of the most important design challenges you will face. Today we examine the psychology that underpins player engagement and learn how to apply it deliberately in our designs.
key_concepts: Self-Determination Theory: autonomy (meaningful choice), competence (mastery progression), and relatedness (social belonging) as core intrinsic motivators
Intrinsic motivation: playing because the activity itself is rewarding; sustained, deep engagement
Extrinsic motivation: playing for external rewards (points, achievements, prizes); effective short-term but fragile long-term
The overjustification effect: providing external rewards for intrinsically motivated behavior can actually reduce intrinsic motivation
Bartle's taxonomy: Achievers (want to master the system), Explorers (want to discover the world), Socializers (want to connect with people), Killers (want to dominate others)
Extensions of Bartle: Quantic Foundry's motivation model adds immersion, creativity, and mastery dimensions
Csikszentmihalyi's flow channel: the zone of engagement where challenge closely matches skill level
Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment: algorithmically scaling challenge to keep each player in their flow channel
Layered challenge design: accessible core path with optional harder objectives for skilled players
The danger of dark patterns: exploiting psychological vulnerabilities for retention at the expense of player well-being
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping player motivation profiles for three contrasting games: World of Warcraft (Achievement/Fellowship), Animal Crossing (Expression/Discovery), and competitive Counter-Strike (Challenge/Competition), then taking Bartle's Player Type quiz as a class.
setup_requirements: Access to Bartle's Player Type quiz online
Design journal for recording personal motivation profile

review.value: A state of deep focused engagement occurring when challenge closely matches player skill. Too easy causes boredom, too hard causes anxiety. The designer's job is to keep the player in the channel between.
Three innate psychological needs drive lasting engagement: autonomy (choice), competence (mastery), and relatedness (connection). Games satisfying all three produce the deepest intrinsic motivation.
Four player types (Achievers, Explorers, Socializers, Killers) each seek fundamentally different experiences from the same game. Designing for only one type alienates the others.
Adding extrinsic rewards (badges, points) to an activity players already enjoy intrinsically can reduce their intrinsic motivation, making them play for the reward rather than the experience.

class_schedule.topic: Game Mechanics and Dynamics in Depth
learning_objectives: Distinguish core mechanics from secondary mechanics and meta-mechanics
Analyze feedback loops (positive and negative) and their effect on game pacing
Design a simple mechanical system on paper and predict its dynamics
Evaluate the depth versus complexity trade-off in mechanical design
readings: Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 1-3
A Game Design Vocabulary by Anna Anthropy & Naomi Clark, Ch. 3
Characteristics of Games by Elias, Garfield & Gutschera, Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction:
In the MDA framework, mechanics are the layer the designer controls directly. But not all mechanics are created equal. The core mechanic is the single repeatable action that defines the moment-to-moment experience: jumping in Mario, matching in Candy Crush, building in Minecraft. Everything else, the power-ups, the scoring, the progression, are secondary mechanics that support, modify, or contextualize the core.

Today we go deeper into how mechanics interact to produce dynamics. When two or more mechanics connect, they form a system, and systems exhibit behaviors that no single mechanic would produce on its own. The most important of these behaviors are feedback loops. A positive feedback loop amplifies advantage: the player who gets ahead gets further ahead (Monopoly's property accumulation). A negative feedback loop dampens advantage: the player who falls behind gets help catching up (Mario Kart's item distribution). The balance between these loops determines whether a game snowballs to a quick conclusion or stays competitive to the finish.

A common trap for beginning designers is conflating complexity with depth. A game with fifty different resource types might be complex without being deep, it just has a lot of stuff. A game like Go has exactly one type of move (place a stone) but produces extraordinary strategic depth. Depth comes from the number of meaningfully different decisions a player faces, not the number of rules.
key_concepts: Core mechanic: the single repeatable action defining moment-to-moment gameplay (jump, match, shoot, place, negotiate)
Secondary mechanics: actions that modify or contextualize the core (power-ups, scoring multipliers, special abilities)
Meta-mechanics: systems operating above individual matches (rankings, seasonal progression, unlockable content)
Positive feedback loops: success breeds success, accelerating toward a conclusion (snowball effects)
Negative feedback loops: mechanisms that slow leaders and help lagging players, stabilizing competition (rubber-banding)
Depth vs. complexity: depth is the number of meaningfully different decisions available; complexity is the number of rules to learn. Ideal designs maximize the ratio of depth to complexity.
Emergent mechanics: behaviors that arise from system interactions but were not explicitly authored (e.g., rocket jumping in Quake)
Mechanical elegance: achieving maximum expressive range from minimum rule count, as in Go, Chess, or Tetris
Feedback loop identification exercise: diagramming the loops in Monopoly, Risk, and Mario Kart to predict pacing
The danger of feature creep: adding mechanics without ensuring they interact meaningfully with the existing system
lecture_notes.demonstration: Collaborative whiteboard exercise diagramming the mechanical systems of Monopoly, identifying all positive and negative feedback loops, and predicting how adding a 'wealth tax' rule would change the dynamics.
setup_requirements: Whiteboard or large paper for system diagrams
Colored markers for distinguishing loop types
Design journal

review.value: The fundamental, repeatable player action around which the entire game is built. Changing the core mechanic changes the game's identity.
A system where gaining an advantage makes gaining further advantage easier, accelerating outcomes. Produces decisive, sometimes unfair, endings.
A system where gaining an advantage triggers counterforces that reduce that advantage, promoting close competition and extended play sessions.
Depth is the number of meaningfully different choices; complexity is the number of rules. Great games maximize the ratio: simple to learn, rich in strategic possibility.
Adding mechanics that do not meaningfully interact with the existing system increases complexity without increasing depth, making the game harder to learn without making it more interesting to play.

learning_objectives: Classify games by genre and identify defining genre conventions for each
Analyze how genre expectations shape player behavior and design assumptions
Evaluate genre-blending as a deliberate design strategy
Describe the lifecycle of a genre from innovation through saturation to reinvention
readings: Game Design Workshop by Tracy Fullerton, Ch. 3
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 16
Blood, Sweat, and Pixels by Jason Schreier, selected chapter on Stardew Valley

lecture_notes.introduction:
Genre is the shared vocabulary between designers and players. When a player picks up a first-person shooter, they expect a crosshair, a health indicator, ammunition management, and an aim-down-sights mechanic. These are not universal truths about game design; they are conventions, patterns that have been refined over decades of iteration within a genre. Understanding genre conventions helps you either satisfy or subvert expectations deliberately.

Subverting genre conventions can produce extraordinary results. Undertale subverts the RPG convention that enemies must be killed by allowing every encounter to be resolved peacefully. Portal subverts the FPS convention that guns deal damage by making the gun create spatial connections. Breath of the Wild subverts the open-world convention of marker-driven quests by replacing them with physics-driven exploration. In each case, the subversion works because the designer deeply understood the convention being broken and had a clear reason for breaking it.

Genres are not static categories; they are living traditions that evolve. Every genre follows a lifecycle: an innovative game establishes a new template, imitators refine the formula, the market becomes saturated with similar titles, and eventually a bold designer reinvents the genre by challenging its core assumptions. Understanding where a genre is in its lifecycle helps you decide whether to work within its conventions or push against them.
key_concepts: Major game genres: Action, Adventure, RPG, Strategy, Simulation, Puzzle, Sports, Fighting, Rhythm, Survival
Genre conventions as implicit contracts with the player: violating conventions without signaling causes confusion, subverting them intentionally creates innovation
Hybrid genres and their design challenges: Roguelite (Hades), Action-RPG (Diablo), Puzzle-Platformer (Baba Is You), Survival-Crafting (Minecraft)
Platform affordances and genre: touch screens favor casual and puzzle genres; gamepads favor action; keyboard/mouse favors strategy and FPS
The genre lifecycle: innovation, refinement, saturation, reinvention, illustrated by the FPS genre from Wolfenstein to Half-Life to Overwatch
Genre expectations and onboarding: players arriving from a known genre bring assumptions that can accelerate or impede learning
The 'de-make' exercise: reducing a complex modern game to its genre-defining essentials reveals which conventions are load-bearing
Audience segmentation by genre preference and its implications for marketing and monetization
Case study: how Stardew Valley refined the farming-sim genre by combining Harvest Moon conventions with modern quality-of-life improvements
When to follow conventions and when to break them: the risk-reward calculus of genre innovation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Genre mapping exercise: placing 20 well-known titles on a genre-convention matrix, identifying which conventions each follows and breaks, and discussing the outliers that defy easy classification.
setup_requirements: Genre reference chart handout (provided)
Design journal for matrix exercise

review.value: Established design patterns within a genre that set player expectations. Health bars in action games, turn order in strategy games, experience points in RPGs are all conventions, not requirements.
Combining mechanics from multiple genres to create novel experiences. Succeeds when the combined mechanics interact synergistically; fails when they create conflicting player expectations.
Innovation establishes a template, refinement optimizes it, saturation commoditizes it, and reinvention disrupts it. Knowing where a genre is in this cycle informs design strategy.
Deliberately breaking a genre convention works only when the designer deeply understands the convention and has a clear design reason for the break. Random deviation reads as a mistake.

learning_objectives: Build a paper prototype of a simple game mechanic within 30 minutes
Identify which design questions a paper prototype can and cannot answer
Apply rapid iteration to refine a core mechanic across multiple playtest cycles
Use the Wizard-of-Oz technique to simulate digital systems with physical materials
readings: Game Design Workshop by Tracy Fullerton, Ch. 8
Challenges for Game Designers by Brenda Brathwaite & Ian Schreiber, Ch. 2
Prototyping a Game in 7 Days (GDC postmortem of Experimental Gameplay Project)

lecture_notes.introduction:
The fastest way to test a game idea is with paper and pencil. Paper prototyping strips a design down to its core mechanic and lets you iterate before writing a single line of code. It is also one of the most humbling exercises in game design, because ideas that sound brilliant in your head often fall apart the moment another human being interacts with them.

Paper prototyping works because it is cheap, fast, and focuses attention on mechanics rather than aesthetics. A beautiful digital prototype can seduce you into thinking the design is better than it is because it looks and sounds good. A paper prototype made from index cards and dice has no such camouflage. If the core mechanic is not engaging when represented by scraps of paper, no amount of polish will save it.

The Wizard-of-Oz technique extends paper prototyping to simulate systems that would normally require code. One player acts as the computer, resolving rules, generating random events, and tracking state manually. This lets you test surprisingly complex systems, AI behavior, procedural generation patterns, network interactions, without writing any software. Today is a workshop day. You will build, playtest, and iterate on a prototype of your own design.
key_concepts: Why prototype on paper: speed (minutes not weeks), cost (pennies not dollars), focus on mechanics over aesthetics, accessibility to non-programmers
Materials toolkit: index cards, dice (d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20), tokens, paper grids, sticky notes, scissors, markers
The Wizard-of-Oz technique: a human simulates the computer, resolving rules and generating events in real time
Identifying the core mechanic and isolating it for testing: strip away everything except the one interaction you need to validate
The rapid iteration cycle: play for 5 minutes, observe, identify the biggest problem, change one thing, play again
What paper prototypes can test: core mechanic engagement, balance, pacing, decision complexity, rule clarity
What paper prototypes cannot test: real-time responsiveness, audio-visual impact, network latency, precise physics
Common paper prototype formats: card games, board games, tile-placement, role-playing scenarios, war games
The 'one-page rules' challenge: if you cannot explain your prototype's rules on a single page, the design is too complex for its current stage
Documenting iterations: photographing each version and writing a one-sentence description of what changed and why
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live construction of a paper prototype for a resource-management mechanic using index cards and dice, followed by a 10-minute playtest with student volunteers and one round of iteration based on observed problems.
setup_requirements: Index cards, dice (multiple types), tokens, scissors, markers (provided in class kit)
Timer for structured 5-minute play sessions
Camera or phone for documenting prototype versions

review.value: A low-fidelity, physical representation of a game mechanic used to test and refine design ideas before committing to digital development. Fast, cheap, and brutally honest.
Stripping a design to its single most important interaction to validate whether that interaction is inherently engaging before adding supporting mechanics.
A person plays the role of the computer, manually resolving rules and generating events, allowing designers to test complex systems without writing code.
Short cycles of play, observe, identify the biggest problem, change one thing, and play again. Volume of iterations matters more than perfection of any single version.

learning_objectives: Plan and conduct a structured playtest session with clear objectives
Distinguish between qualitative and quantitative playtest data and know when to use each
Write neutral observation questions that avoid leading the tester
Analyze playtest data to identify actionable design insights
readings: Game Design Workshop by Tracy Fullerton, Ch. 9-10
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 24
Game Usability: Advancing the Player Experience, Ch. 2 (Desurvire & Wiberg)

lecture_notes.introduction:
A game that is never tested with real players is a game designed for an audience of one. Playtesting is not a quality assurance step that happens at the end of development; it is a continuous design tool that should begin the moment you have anything playable, even a paper prototype.

The most important skill in playtesting is observation. What players say about their experience is valuable, but what they actually do is far more revealing. Players will tell you a puzzle was 'pretty easy' while you watch them fail it seven times. They will say they 'liked the combat' while spending most of their time avoiding it. The gap between reported experience and observed behavior is where the most actionable design insights live.

There are many types of playtests, each suited to a different stage of development and a different set of questions. Self-testing catches obvious bugs and logic errors. Internal testing with teammates reveals balance problems and unclear rules. External testing with strangers reveals learnability issues and assumption gaps. Formal usability testing with think-aloud protocols reveals the precise moment-by-moment thought process of the player. Today we learn how to plan, conduct, and analyze each type.
key_concepts: Types of playtests: self-testing, internal team testing, closed external testing, open beta testing, formal usability testing
The think-aloud protocol: asking players to verbalize their thought process as they play, revealing decision-making and confusion in real time
Writing neutral observation questions: 'What were you trying to do?' not 'Did you like the new weapon?'
The observation-versus-feedback distinction: watch what players do (objective) rather than only listening to what they say (subjective)
Quantitative metrics: completion rate, time-on-task, error frequency, retry count, heatmaps of player position
Qualitative data: think-aloud transcripts, post-session interviews, open-ended survey responses, body language notes
Sample size guidance: 5 testers reveal ~85% of usability problems; larger samples needed for balance data
The playtest facilitator role: remain neutral, do not help, do not explain, do not defend the design
Common playtest mistakes: explaining the game before letting players try it, asking leading questions, testing with friends who want to be polite
Turning playtest data into design action: prioritizing findings by severity and frequency, generating hypotheses, planning targeted changes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Facilitated playtest of student paper prototypes from Week 7, with half the class playing and half observing and recording data, followed by group analysis of findings.
setup_requirements: Paper prototypes from Week 7
Playtest observation forms (provided)
Stopwatch or timer for tracking time-on-task
Consent forms for recording if applicable

review.value: What players do (struggling with a puzzle for 5 minutes) is more reliable data than what they say ('it was fine'). Always prioritize observed behavior over reported experience.
Asking players to narrate their thought process while playing reveals moment-by-moment decision-making, confusion points, and expectation mismatches in real time.
Ask 'what were you trying to do there?' instead of 'did you like that feature?' Leading questions produce confirmatory data that tells you nothing useful.
Nielsen's research shows that five testers typically reveal approximately 85% of usability problems. You do not need large samples for qualitative insights.

learning_objectives: Outline the standard sections and purpose of a game design document
Write a clear game overview that communicates the core vision in one page
Document mechanics precisely enough for a programmer to implement them without ambiguity
Maintain the GDD as a living document that evolves with the design
readings: Game Design Workshop by Tracy Fullerton, Ch. 14
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 25
One Page Designs: How to Write a Pitch (Ubisoft GDD template, available online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
The game design document is the central reference that communicates your game's vision, mechanics, content, and scope to every member of the development team. A programmer reads it to know what to build. An artist reads it to know what to draw. A producer reads it to know how to plan. A publisher reads it to decide whether to fund it.

The GDD is not a sacred text written once and followed forever. It is a living document that evolves as the game is prototyped, playtested, and iterated. The best GDDs are concise, well-organized, and precise. They answer the question 'what does this game do?' without drowning the reader in unnecessary detail. The worst GDDs are hundred-page novels that nobody reads, filled with vague descriptions and aspirational language that gives no actionable guidance.

Writing a good GDD requires a different skill than designing a good game. You must communicate mechanical systems unambiguously, describe aesthetic goals concretely, and scope content realistically. Today we examine professional GDD templates, practice writing key sections, and discuss how to maintain the document as the game changes.
key_concepts: GDD purpose: a shared reference that aligns the team around a single vision and provides enough detail for implementation without over-specifying
Standard sections: game overview (one-page pitch), core mechanics, game flow, level design, art direction, audio design, UI/UX, technical requirements, content list, project timeline
The one-page game overview: genre, platform, target audience, core mechanic, unique selling proposition, comparable titles, and aesthetic goals
Mechanic documentation: input, rule, output format with edge cases and examples for every interaction
Content matrices: spreadsheets listing every enemy, item, level, and ability with their parameters for balance tracking
Art direction section: mood boards, color palettes, style references, and technical specifications (resolution, file formats)
The living document principle: version control, changelog, and flagging sections as 'approved,' 'in progress,' or 'needs revision'
Common GDD failures: too long to read, too vague to implement, never updated after initial draft, written by one person without team input
Pitch documents versus full GDDs: the pitch sells the idea in 1-5 pages; the full GDD provides implementation detail
Using the GDD as a project management tool: mapping sections to milestones and tracking completion percentage
lecture_notes.demonstration: Collaborative writing exercise where the class writes a one-page game overview for an imaginary game, then expands one mechanic into a full mechanical specification with input/rule/output format, edge cases, and examples.
setup_requirements: Laptop or tablet for document writing
GDD template (provided on course page)
Access to shared document platform (Google Docs or equivalent)

review.value: A living reference document that communicates the game's vision, mechanics, content, and scope to all team members. Must be precise enough to implement, concise enough to read.

Genre, platform, target audience, core mechanic, unique selling proposition, comparable titles, and aesthetic goals, all on a single page. If you cannot pitch it in one page, the vision is not clear enough.

Each mechanic needs: what input triggers it, what rule processes it, what output results, and what edge cases exist. If a programmer cannot implement it from your description alone, it is not specific enough.
A GDD that never changes after the first draft is a GDD nobody uses. Version control it, maintain a changelog, and flag sections by status.

learning_objectives: Apply key level design principles including training, pacing, gating, and spatial storytelling
Design a level layout on paper that teaches a mechanic through play rather than text
Use difficulty curves and safe spaces to control player pacing
Critique existing levels using a structured evaluation framework
readings: Level Up! The Guide to Great Video Game Design by Scott Rogers, Ch. 9-11
An Architectural Approach to Level Design by Christopher Totten, Ch. 1-3
Nintendo's Approach to Level Design (GDC talk on World 1-1 analysis)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Level design is the art of arranging space, obstacles, rewards, and enemies to create a structured experience within a game's mechanical framework. A well-designed level teaches the player its rules through play, escalates challenge at an appropriate pace, rewards exploration, and tells a spatial story without a single line of dialogue.

The gold standard of level design education is World 1-1 of Super Mario Bros. In approximately 30 seconds of gameplay, it teaches the player that question blocks are interactive, that mushrooms are beneficial, that goombas are dangerous, and that pipes can be entered, all without a tutorial screen or text prompt. Every element is placed to guide the player's natural curiosity and experimentation. This kind of environmental teaching is called 'conveyance,' and it is the most elegant form of onboarding.

Level design is also the primary tool for controlling pacing. A long corridor of enemies creates tension. A quiet garden with a save point provides relief. A locked door requiring a key found elsewhere creates a puzzle loop that extends play time and rewards spatial memory. The arrangement of these elements determines the emotional rhythm of the experience, and getting that rhythm right is what separates a memorable level from a forgettable one.
key_concepts: Conveyance: teaching the player through environmental design rather than explicit tutorials, using placement, visibility, and progressive complexity
The 'introduce, develop, twist' pattern: present a mechanic in a safe context, let the player practice it, then combine it with another element for a challenge
Gating: using skill gates, item gates, knowledge gates, or narrative gates to control player progression through the level
Pacing control: alternating tension (combat, puzzles, time pressure) with relief (safe rooms, vistas, rewards) to prevent fatigue
Spatial storytelling: communicating narrative through environment details (a wrecked room tells a story of conflict) without text or cutscenes
Sight lines and landmarks: placing visible landmarks so players can orient themselves and set goals ('I want to reach that tower')
Critical path vs. optional content: the main route through the level versus hidden areas, secrets, and side challenges
The kiss/kill principle: every element in a level should either help the player (kiss) or challenge them (kill); neutral elements waste the player's attention
Paper level design: sketching layouts with annotations for enemy placement, item locations, camera positions, and intended player flow
lecture_notes.demonstration: Detailed structural analysis of Super Mario Bros. World 1-1 element by element, then a paper level design workshop where students sketch a level that teaches a new mechanic using conveyance principles.
setup_requirements: Graph paper or digital drawing tool for level layouts
Colored pencils or markers for annotations
Access to a Mario 1-1 playthrough video for reference

review.value: Teaching the player through environmental design rather than text tutorials. The ideal level teaches its rules through carefully arranged encounters that guide natural experimentation.
Present a mechanic safely, let the player practice, then combine it with something new. This three-beat structure is the foundation of progressive level design.
Alternating between tension and relief prevents fatigue and creates emotional rhythm. Too much tension exhausts; too much relief bores.
Using environment details to communicate narrative without words. A bloodstain on a wall, a child's toy in a ruined house, footprints leading to a cliff edge, all tell stories through space.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between embedded narrative (authored story) and emergent narrative (player-generated story)
Analyze narrative structures suited to interactive media (branching, modular, environmental)
Evaluate the ludonarrative consonance and dissonance in specific games
Write dialogue and scenario descriptions for a branching narrative sequence
readings: The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 17-18
Interactive Storytelling for Video Games by Josiah Lebowitz, Ch. 1-3
Hamlet on the Holodeck by Janet Murray, Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction:
Narrative in games operates under fundamentally different rules than narrative in film or literature. In a movie, the director controls every second of the viewer's experience. In a game, the player has agency, the ability to make choices that affect the outcome. This agency is both the greatest strength and the greatest challenge of interactive narrative.

There are two broad categories of game narrative. Embedded narrative is the story the designer authors: cutscenes, dialogue trees, scripted events, environmental lore. Emergent narrative is the story the player creates through their actions: the desperate last-stand battle, the unlikely alliance, the accidental discovery that changed everything. The best narrative games weave both types together, providing enough authored structure to give the experience meaning while leaving enough freedom for the player to feel like the story is truly theirs.

A critical concept is ludonarrative consonance and dissonance. Consonance occurs when the mechanics reinforce the narrative themes: in Papers, Please, the tedious stamp-checking mechanic makes you feel the oppressive monotony of bureaucratic work, which is the game's thematic point. Dissonance occurs when mechanics contradict the narrative: in many action games, the story portrays the protagonist as a reluctant hero while gameplay requires them to kill hundreds of enemies. Understanding this relationship helps you design games where mechanics and narrative push in the same direction.
key_concepts: Embedded narrative: authored content including cutscenes, dialogue, lore documents, scripted sequences, and environmental storytelling
Emergent narrative: unscripted stories generated by player actions and system interactions (Dwarf Fortress, RimWorld, player anecdotes from open-world games)
Branching narrative: decision points where the player's choice leads to different story paths, with consequences that may converge or diverge permanently
Modular narrative: self-contained story modules (quests, encounters) that can be experienced in any order, with an optional overarching thread
Environmental storytelling: communicating narrative through space, objects, and ambient details rather than text or dialogue
Ludonarrative consonance: when game mechanics reinforce and express the narrative themes (Papers, Please, Celeste, Hades)
Ludonarrative dissonance: when mechanics contradict the narrative (a story about peace told through combat mechanics)
The agency-authorship spectrum: linear games (high authorship, low agency) to sandboxes (low authorship, high agency) with various hybrid points
Character development through mechanics: leveling, relationship meters, and moral alignment systems as narrative tools
Writing for interactivity: dialogue must account for multiple contexts, player knowledge states, and conversation re-entry
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of narrative approaches in three games: The Last of Us (linear embedded), Disco Elysium (branching dialogue), and Dwarf Fortress (emergent), identifying how each balances authorship and agency.
setup_requirements: Design journal for narrative analysis exercise
Access to dialogue tree diagramming tool (Twine, Ink, or pen and paper)

review.value: Embedded narrative is authored by the designer; emergent narrative is created by the player through gameplay. The strongest narrative games combine both.
When mechanics and narrative reinforce each other, the experience feels unified and the themes land with greater impact.
Player choices lead to different story paths. The challenge is making choices feel meaningful without multiplying content requirements exponentially.
Using space and objects to communicate narrative. A room tells its story through what is present, what is missing, and how things are arranged.

learning_objectives: Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric game balance
Apply transitive and intransitive relationship models to balance design
Use spreadsheet modeling to test balance hypotheses quantitatively
Identify common balance problems and propose mechanical solutions
readings: Game Balance by Ian Schreiber & Brenda Romero, Ch. 1-4
Game Mechanics: Advanced Game Design by Adams & Dormans, Ch. 6
Sirlin's 'Balancing Multiplayer Games' series (online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Balance is the art of making a game fair without making it boring. A perfectly balanced game where every option is identical offers no interesting choices. A badly balanced game where one option dominates offers only one choice. The sweet spot is a game where multiple viable strategies exist, each with distinct strengths and weaknesses, so that the player's skill in choosing and executing a strategy determines the outcome.

Balance operates on multiple levels simultaneously. Symmetric balance means all players start with identical resources and options (Chess, Go). Asymmetric balance means players have different abilities, units, or resources but roughly equal chances of winning (StarCraft's three factions). Asymmetric balance is harder to achieve but produces more varied and replayable experiences because each player's game feels different.

The two fundamental relationship types in balance are transitive and intransitive. Transitive relationships form strict hierarchies: a Steel Sword is always better than an Iron Sword. Intransitive relationships form circular dominance: Rock beats Scissors, Scissors beats Paper, Paper beats Rock. Transitive relationships create clear progression and reward investment. Intransitive relationships prevent dominant strategies and encourage adaptation. Most well-balanced games use both.
key_concepts: Symmetric balance: identical starting conditions for all players (Chess, Go, Checkers)
Asymmetric balance: different abilities or resources with equivalent winning chances (StarCraft factions, Overwatch heroes, Settlers of Catan starting positions)
Transitive relationships: strict power hierarchies where A is always better than B (upgrade paths, tier lists)
Intransitive relationships: circular dominance where A beats B, B beats C, C beats A, preventing any single dominant strategy
Cost curves: assigning numerical costs to abilities/units proportional to their power level, then checking that nothing provides outsized value for its cost
Dominant strategies: when one option is always the best choice regardless of context, the game has a balance problem
Spreadsheet modeling: simulating hundreds of encounters between units/strategies to find statistical outliers
Perceived fairness vs. mathematical fairness: players judge balance by feel, not by statistics; a mathematically fair system that feels unfair is effectively unfair
Patch culture: the modern practice of ongoing balance adjustments post-launch based on player data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on spreadsheet exercise analyzing StarCraft II unit cost-efficiency: calculating damage-per-resource-spent for Terran, Protoss, and Zerg units and identifying the most cost-efficient compositions.
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet application (Google Sheets, Excel)
Unit data reference sheet (provided)
Calculator for cost-efficiency ratios

review.value: Giving players different abilities while ensuring roughly equal chances of winning. Harder to achieve than symmetric balance but produces more varied experiences.
Circular dominance (A beats B, B beats C, C beats A) that prevents any single option from being universally dominant. The foundation of counter-play.
When one option is always the best choice regardless of opponent behavior. If a dominant strategy exists, the game effectively has no meaningful decisions.
A system that is statistically fair but feels unfair to players is a design failure. Balance must satisfy both the math and the player's subjective experience.

learning_objectives: Identify common accessibility barriers in games across visual, auditory, motor, and cognitive dimensions
Apply accessibility guidelines to a game design without compromising the core experience
Evaluate existing games for accessibility using the Game Accessibility Guidelines framework
Design inclusive features that benefit all players, not only those with disabilities
readings: Game Accessibility Guidelines (gameaccessibilityguidelines.com), Basic and Intermediate tiers
The Last of Us Part II Accessibility Case Study (Naughty Dog GDC presentation)
Includification by AbleGamers Foundation, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction:
Accessibility in game design means removing unnecessary barriers so that the widest possible audience can experience your game. This is not about making every game playable by every person in every situation; it is about making deliberate choices about which barriers are essential to the experience and which are incidental obstacles that exclude players for no design reason.

Consider colorblindness, which affects roughly 8% of men and 0.5% of women worldwide. A puzzle game that requires distinguishing red from green gems excludes a significant portion of its potential audience for no reason if the same mechanic could work with shapes, symbols, or labeled colors. Adding a colorblind mode is not difficult and does not change the game for anyone who does not need it.

The strongest argument for accessibility is that accessible features benefit everyone. Subtitles help deaf players, but they also help players in noisy environments. Remappable controls help players with motor disabilities, but they also help left-handed players and players who prefer non-standard layouts. Adjustable difficulty helps players with cognitive disabilities, but it also helps parents who only have 20 minutes to play. The Last of Us Part II shipped with over 60 accessibility options and received universal acclaim, demonstrating that accessibility and design excellence are not in conflict.
key_concepts: Visual accessibility: colorblind modes, high-contrast options, scalable UI text, screen reader support, audio descriptions
Auditory accessibility: subtitles with speaker identification, visual sound indicators, vibration cues, music visualization
Motor accessibility: remappable controls, adjustable input timing, one-handed control schemes, auto-aim options, toggle vs. hold
Cognitive accessibility: difficulty options, clear objective markers, journal systems, adjustable game speed, simplified control modes
The curb-cut effect: features designed for accessibility (subtitles, remappable controls) benefit all players in various situations
Essential vs. incidental difficulty: a dark horror game needs darkness for its aesthetic, but requiring precise color discrimination adds nothing to the horror
The Game Accessibility Guidelines framework: basic, intermediate, and advanced tiers organized by disability category
Cost-benefit analysis: many accessibility features (remappable keys, subtitle options, font size scaling) are low-effort and high-impact
Accessibility as a design constraint: working within accessibility guidelines often produces more creative solutions, not less creative ones
Legal and market considerations: accessibility requirements in government procurement, the growing size of the accessible gaming market
lecture_notes.demonstration: Accessibility audit of a popular game using the Game Accessibility Guidelines checklist, identifying which barriers are essential to the experience and which could be removed with minimal effort.
setup_requirements: Printed Game Accessibility Guidelines checklist (provided)
Access to a game to audit (each student brings their own or uses a provided title)
Design journal for audit notes

review.value: Accessibility features designed for people with disabilities frequently benefit all users. Subtitles help deaf players and players in noisy rooms. Remappable controls help players with motor impairments and players who prefer different layouts.

Difficulty that is core to the design intent (Dark Souls' challenge) should be preserved. Difficulty that is an unintended side effect of a design choice (unreadable font, color-only indicators) should be removed.
Visual, auditory, motor, and cognitive. Each dimension has specific barriers and specific solutions. A comprehensive accessibility approach addresses all four.
Remappable controls, subtitle options, scalable font sizes, and colorblind modes are inexpensive to implement and open the game to significantly more players.

class_schedule.topic: The Game Industry: Roles, Pipelines, and Career Paths
learning_objectives: Describe the major roles in a game development team and their responsibilities
Outline the phases of a game development pipeline from concept to post-launch
Compare career paths in AAA studios, indie development, and adjacent industries
Prepare a portfolio piece and elevator pitch for a game design role
readings: Blood, Sweat, and Pixels by Jason Schreier, Ch. 1 (Pillars of Eternity)
The Game Industry Map (IGDA resource on roles and career paths)
Designing Your Career in Games by Brenda Romero (GDC talk)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Understanding the game industry is essential for anyone considering a career in game design. The industry is enormous, generating more revenue than film and music combined, but it is also demanding, competitive, and rapidly changing. Knowing how the business works, who does what, and how games actually get made will help you navigate your career and set realistic expectations.

A modern AAA game involves hundreds of people across dozens of disciplines. The design team creates the game's systems, levels, and content. The engineering team builds the technology. The art team creates the visual assets. The audio team provides sound and music. The production team manages schedules, budgets, and communication. The QA team tests everything. Understanding how these teams interact, where handoffs happen, and what each team needs from the others, is what separates a game designer who ships successful products from one who creates beautiful documents that never become real games.

The indie path offers a different trade-off: creative freedom in exchange for financial risk. Indie developers often wear multiple hats, doing design, programming, art, and marketing for a single project. The skills you learn in this course, prototyping, playtesting, documentation, analytical thinking, are equally valuable whether you join a 500-person studio or start a two-person team in your garage.
key_concepts: Core development roles: game designer, level designer, systems designer, narrative designer, programmer, artist, animator, audio designer, producer, QA tester
The development pipeline: concept and pre-production, vertical slice, alpha, beta, gold master, launch, live operations
Pre-production deliverables: game design document, art bible, technical design document, project plan, prototype
Production methodologies: Waterfall (sequential), Agile/Scrum (iterative sprints), and hybrid approaches common in game studios
AAA vs. indie vs. AA: team size, budget, creative freedom, financial risk, and career stability trade-offs
Adjacent careers: gamification designer, UX researcher, esports organizer, game journalist, educator, tools developer
Portfolio essentials for a game designer: documented design work, playable prototypes, analytical writing, and demonstrated iteration
The elevator pitch: a 30-second verbal summary of your game concept that communicates genre, core mechanic, unique hook, and target audience
Crunch culture and labor issues: industry awareness of working conditions, unionization movements, and sustainable development practices
Networking and community: GDC, game jams (Global Game Jam, Ludum Dare), local IGDA chapters, and online communities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mock pitch session where students practice delivering 30-second elevator pitches for their final project concepts, followed by peer feedback on clarity, enthusiasm, and specificity.
setup_requirements: Prepared elevator pitch for your game concept
Portfolio examples or work-in-progress to share
Notepad for peer feedback notes

review.value: Concept, pre-production, vertical slice, full production, alpha (feature-complete), beta (content-complete), gold master (ship-ready), launch, live operations.
Creating the vision, documenting the systems, communicating across teams, playtesting continuously, and iterating based on data. The designer is the advocate for the player experience.
Show your process, not just your results. Documented design iterations, playtest data, and before/after comparisons demonstrate the analytical thinking studios value most.
A 30-second verbal summary that communicates genre, core mechanic, unique selling point, and target audience. If you cannot pitch it in 30 seconds, the concept needs sharper focus.

learning_objectives: Present a complete game design document with confidence and clarity
Receive and integrate peer feedback constructively
Reflect on personal growth across the semester and identify areas for continued development
Synthesize course concepts into a coherent personal design philosophy
readings: Review all course materials and your design journal entries
Presentation Best Practices for Game Designers (GDC resource, available online)

lecture_notes.introduction:
This final session is your opportunity to present the game design document you have been developing throughout the semester. Each of you will present your game concept, walk the class through your design decisions, and field questions from your peers and from me. Treat this as a professional pitch; the ability to communicate your vision clearly and persuasively is as important as the design itself.

After presentations, we will conduct a course retrospective. We will revisit the core frameworks, MDA, flow, player types, genre conventions, level design principles, and discuss how your understanding of each has evolved since Week 1. I will ask each of you to articulate your personal design philosophy: the principles and priorities that guide your design decisions. There is no single correct answer; every designer develops their own perspective through practice and reflection.

Finally, we will discuss next steps. Whether you continue into GD102, pursue programming in CS115, develop your art skills in GD103, or start building games on your own, the analytical tools and iterative mindset you have developed in this course will serve you in every path forward. The game design journey does not end with a course; it ends when you stop being curious about why games work.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: hook the audience, state the vision, walk through the core mechanic, show iteration evidence, discuss playtesting results, conclude with next steps
Handling questions: listen fully before answering, acknowledge valid criticism, defend decisions with evidence not emotion, note suggestions for future iteration
Peer feedback protocols: specific, actionable, balanced (what works, what could improve, one question for the designer to consider)
Personal design philosophy: a concise statement of the principles and priorities that guide your design decisions

Course synthesis: connecting MDA framework, flow theory, player motivation, genre conventions, prototyping, playtesting, level design, narrative, balance, and accessibility into a unified design toolkit
Growth reflection: comparing your Week 1 design journal entry to your final GDD to measure how your thinking has evolved
Next steps: advanced courses (GD102, GD103, CS115), game jams, independent projects, internships, portfolio development
The designer's mindset: every game you play is a design lesson; develop the habit of asking 'why does this work?' and 'what would happen if this changed?'
Community and continued learning: joining IGDA, attending game jams, reading postmortems, following GDC talks, building a professional network
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student presentations (8-10 minutes each) followed by 5 minutes of peer and instructor Q&A per presentation.
setup_requirements: Final game design document (printed and digital copies)
Presentation slides or visual aids
Playable prototype if available (paper or digital)

review.value: A great game design means nothing if you cannot communicate it clearly. Practice your pitch, know your material, and be prepared to defend your decisions with evidence.
Your personal set of principles about what matters most in game design. Developed through practice, reflection, and studying the work of others.
Game design is a craft refined through practice. Play games analytically, prototype constantly, seek feedback relentlessly, and never stop asking why things work.

MDA gives you vocabulary. Flow gives you difficulty targets. Player types give you audience awareness. Prototyping and playtesting give you validation. The GDD gives you communication. Together they form a complete design toolkit.

quizzes.topic: Game Definitions, MDA Framework, and Design Foundations

questions.question: Using the MDA framework, explain how the same mechanic (limited inventory space) creates different aesthetics in a survival horror game versus a cozy farming simulation. Be specific about which aesthetics are produced in each case and why.
questions.answer: In survival horror, limited inventory creates tension and difficult resource-management decisions (aesthetics of Challenge and Submission), forcing players to choose between weapons and healing items under threat of death. The dynamics include anxious deliberation, risk assessment, and the sunk-cost of discarding items. In a cozy farming sim, the same mechanic creates a gentle prioritization puzzle about which crops or gifts to carry (aesthetics of Discovery and Expression), encouraging strategic trip planning without life-or-death consequences. The dynamics differ because surrounding mechanics (combat danger vs. relaxed timers) fundamentally change the emotional context. This demonstrates MDA's core insight: the same mechanic produces different dynamics and aesthetics depending on the system it exists within.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies contrasting aesthetics for each genre with specific labels from the eight aesthetics
Explains how surrounding mechanics alter the dynamics that arise from the shared mechanic
Uses MDA terminology accurately and demonstrates understanding of the framework's core insight

questions.question: Name three elements that all definitions of 'game' share (across Huizinga, Caillois, Costikyan, Schell, and Salen/Zimmerman) and explain why each is essential. Then identify one element that at least one definition includes but another excludes, and explain the disagreement.
questions.answer: Three shared elements: (1) Rules that constrain behavior, essential because without rules there is no structure distinguishing a game from free play. (2) Goals that direct effort, essential because goals create purpose and provide a measure of success. (3) Voluntary participation, essential because forced play is not play; the magic circle requires consent to enter. One disagreement: Salen/Zimmerman require a 'quantifiable outcome' (win/lose/score), while Costikyan's definition focuses on 'pursuit of a goal' without requiring quantification. This matters because some experiences (sandbox games, creative tools) have goals but no quantifiable outcome, and whether they count as 'games' depends on which definition you use.

rubric.key: Three shared elements correctly identified with clear explanations of why each is essential
Identifies a genuine point of disagreement between specific definitions with named authors
Explains the practical implication of the disagreement for categorizing edge cases

questions.question: A game designer says: 'I added 15 new mechanics to my game to make it more interesting, but playtesters say it feels overwhelming and confusing.' Using the concept of depth versus complexity, diagnose the problem and propose a specific design approach to fix it.
questions.answer: The designer has increased complexity (the number of rules to learn) without necessarily increasing depth (the number of meaningfully different decisions). Fifteen mechanics likely overwhelm the player's cognitive capacity, especially if many do not interact with each other. Diagnosis: the game has high complexity but possibly low depth because mechanics operate independently rather than creating synergistic interactions. Fix: Remove mechanics that do not interact meaningfully with the core mechanic. Keep the 3-5 mechanics that produce the most interesting decisions when combined. Fewer, more interconnected mechanics create deeper gameplay because each mechanic multiplies the decision space of the others, rather than just adding to a list of things to remember.

rubric.key: Correctly applies depth vs. complexity distinction to the scenario
Diagnoses the root cause (independent mechanics not interacting) rather than just restating the symptom
Proposes a specific, actionable fix with reasoning

questions.question: Define Csikszentmihalyi's concept of 'flow' and describe two specific, distinct design techniques a game designer can use to keep players in the flow channel. For each technique, name a published game that demonstrates it.
questions.answer: Flow is a state of deep, focused engagement that occurs when the level of challenge closely matches the player's skill level. If challenge exceeds skill, the player experiences anxiety; if skill exceeds challenge, the player experiences boredom. Technique 1: Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA), where the game monitors performance metrics and scales challenge in real time. Resident Evil 4 adjusts enemy aggressiveness and item drop rates based on how often the player dies or takes damage, keeping both skilled and struggling players in their flow channel. Technique 2: Layered challenge, where the core path is accessible to average players while optional objectives provide escalating challenge. Celeste provides accessible platforming with an assist mode, but also offers extremely difficult B-Side and C-Side levels for players who want greater challenge, letting each player find their own flow channel.

rubric.key: Accurate definition of flow including the challenge-skill balance mechanism
First technique is specific, practical, and supported by a real game example
Second technique is distinct from the first, specific, and supported by a real game example

questions.question: Explain the difference between a positive feedback loop and a negative feedback loop in a game system. For each, give a specific example from a published game, identify the mechanic that creates the loop, and explain whether the loop helps or hurts the player experience in that game.
questions.answer: A positive feedback loop amplifies advantage: success makes further success easier, accelerating toward an endpoint. Example: In Monopoly, owning properties generates rent income, which funds purchasing more properties, which generates more rent. This positive loop hurts the experience because once a player gains an early lead, the gap widens relentlessly, creating a long, boring endgame for losing players who have no realistic path to recovery. A negative feedback loop dampens advantage: gaining a lead triggers mechanisms that slow the leader or help trailing players. Example: In Mario Kart, item distribution is weighted so that players in last place receive powerful items (blue shells, stars) while first-place players receive weak items (bananas, coins). This negative loop helps the experience by keeping races competitive and exciting until the final moments, though it can frustrate skilled players who feel punished for leading.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of both loop types with clear distinction between amplifying and dampening effects
Both examples identify the specific mechanic creating the loop, not just naming the game
Evaluates whether each loop helps or hurts the player experience with reasoning

questions.question: A mobile game rewards players with a rare item after every 100 enemies defeated (fixed-ratio schedule). The design team wants to increase player retention. Using principles of behavioral psychology, propose an alternative reward schedule and explain why it would be more effective at sustaining engagement. Then identify one ethical concern with your proposed change.
questions.answer: Replace the fixed-ratio schedule with a variable-ratio schedule: instead of rewarding every 100th enemy, make the rare item drop randomly with a 1% chance per enemy defeated. This is more effective because variable-ratio reinforcement produces the highest response rates and strongest habit formation. The unpredictability of the reward creates anticipation with every encounter, and the intermittent delivery prevents the reward from becoming routine. Ethical concern: variable-ratio schedules exploit the same psychological mechanisms as slot machines and gambling. Players may continue playing not because they are enjoying the gameplay but because they are chasing the next drop. This is especially concerning in games with microtransactions, where players can spend real money to increase their chances, blurring the line between game engagement and exploitation of compulsive behavior.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies variable-ratio as more effective and explains the psychological mechanism
Explains why variable-ratio produces stronger engagement than fixed-ratio with reference to unpredictability and intermittent reinforcement
Identifies a specific, substantive ethical concern with reasoning

questions.question: A playtest reveals that 80% of testers quit your puzzle game at Level 3. List three specific hypotheses for why this might happen and describe one data-gathering method for each hypothesis that would confirm or refute it.
questions.answer: Hypothesis 1: The difficulty spike between Level 2 and Level 3 is too steep. Method: Record completion times for each level and graph the difficulty curve; a sharp time increase at Level 3 confirms the spike. Hypothesis 2: The puzzle mechanic introduced in Level 3 is poorly tutorialized, so players do not understand what to do. Method: Conduct think-aloud playtests and note where players express confusion or attempt incorrect solutions repeatedly; high confusion frequency at the mechanic introduction confirms poor conveyance. Hypothesis 3: Players lose interest because the core mechanic feels repetitive by Level 3. Method: Post-session survey asking players to rate enjoyment per level and state their primary reason for stopping; if 'boredom' or 'repetitive' appears frequently, the hypothesis is supported.

rubric.key: Three distinct, plausible hypotheses addressing different possible root causes
Each data-gathering method logically matches its hypothesis and would provide actionable evidence
Methods include a mix of quantitative and qualitative approaches

questions.question: Explain why a paper prototype is more useful than a digital prototype in the early stages of design. Then describe one specific design question that a paper prototype cannot answer and explain what type of prototype is needed instead.
questions.answer: Paper prototypes are more useful early because they are fast (minutes to build, not weeks), cheap (paper and markers, not development tools), and force focus on mechanics rather than aesthetics. A beautiful digital prototype can seduce designers into thinking the design is better than it is because it looks and sounds good. Paper strips away that camouflage and exposes whether the core mechanic is inherently engaging. A design question paper cannot answer: 'Does the game's real-time combat feel responsive and satisfying?' Real-time responsiveness depends on frame rate, input latency, animation timing, and audio feedback, none of which paper can simulate. A digital prototype built in a game engine (even a minimal one) is needed to test the feel of real-time interactions.

rubric.key: Explains at least two advantages of paper prototypes with clear reasoning
Identifies a specific design question that genuinely requires digital testing
Explains what type of alternative prototype is needed and why

questions.question: You are facilitating a playtest and a tester says: 'I think you should add a minimap, the level was confusing.' Explain why this feedback, taken at face value, could lead you to the wrong design solution. What follow-up question would you ask, and what alternative solution might the data reveal?
questions.answer: Taking this feedback at face value is dangerous because the tester is prescribing a solution (add a minimap) rather than describing the problem (they got lost). The tester may not understand the actual root cause of their confusion. A minimap might help, but the real problem could be poor landmark design, identical-looking corridors, missing sight lines, or unclear level layout rather than the absence of a specific UI element. Follow-up question: 'Can you tell me about a specific moment when you felt lost? What were you looking for, and what did you try?' This reveals the actual pain point. Alternative solution the data might reveal: if players consistently get lost at a specific fork, the problem might be solved by adding a distinctive landmark at that fork rather than adding an entirely new UI system. Environmental solutions are often more elegant than UI band-aids.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the problem-solution distinction in player feedback
Asks a neutral follow-up question that seeks to understand the root problem
Proposes a plausible alternative solution that addresses the root cause differently

quizzes.topic: Level Design, Narrative, and Player Experience

questions.question: Explain the 'introduce, develop, twist' pattern in level design and describe how Super Mario Bros. World 1-1 applies this pattern to teach the player about the Goomba enemy without any text tutorial.
questions.answer: The 'introduce, develop, twist' pattern presents a game element in a safe, simple context first (introduce), gives the player space to practice interacting with it (develop), then combines it with another element to create a new challenge (twist). In World 1-1, the first Goomba is introduced walking toward the player in an open area with no pits or other hazards, so the only thing the player needs to learn is that this enemy is dangerous and can be defeated by jumping on it. The development occurs as more Goombas appear in slightly more complex arrangements. The twist comes when Goombas appear near pipes or pits, combining the enemy avoidance skill with platforming navigation. Critically, the first Goomba encounter is positioned so that a player running forward naturally jumps (due to a question block overhead), accidentally discovering the defeat mechanic through environmental cueing rather than text instructions.

rubric.key: Correctly defines the three-beat pattern with clear descriptions of each phase
Accurately describes how World 1-1 applies each phase to the Goomba introduction
Notes the environmental cueing that guides the player to discover the mechanic naturally

questions.question: Define ludonarrative consonance and dissonance. Give one example of each from a published game and explain what makes it consonant or dissonant.
questions.answer: Ludonarrative consonance occurs when a game's mechanics reinforce and express its narrative themes. Example: In Papers, Please, the core mechanic is repetitive bureaucratic document checking. This tedious, error-prone process makes the player feel the oppressive monotony that the narrative is about. The mechanic IS the story's theme made interactive, creating deep consonance. Ludonarrative dissonance occurs when mechanics contradict the narrative. Example: In the Uncharted series, the story portrays Nathan Drake as a charming, likeable everyman, but gameplay requires the player to kill hundreds of enemies in every chapter. The narrative says 'relatable hero'; the mechanics say 'mass murderer.' This disconnect undermines the characterization because the player's actions do not match the person the cutscenes describe.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of both consonance and dissonance
Consonance example clearly demonstrates mechanics reinforcing narrative theme with specific detail
Dissonance example clearly demonstrates mechanics contradicting narrative with specific detail

questions.question: You are designing a metroidvania where the player gains new movement abilities (wall jump, dash, double jump) throughout the game. Explain how you would use gating to control progression, and describe how you would design the moment when a player acquires the wall jump ability to maximize the feeling of empowerment.
questions.answer: Gating in a metroidvania uses ability requirements to block access to areas. Before gaining wall jump, the player sees walls they cannot climb and ledges they cannot reach, creating visible but inaccessible goals. After gaining wall jump, these previously blocked paths open simultaneously, rewarding the player with a burst of new exploration options.
