To maximize empowerment when acquiring wall jump: (1) Place the ability room adjacent to a previously impassable wall, so the player immediately uses the new ability in the exact context where they were previously blocked. (2) Design the area accessible via wall jump to contain a clear vertical space with multiple wall-jump surfaces, giving the player a playground to practice the ability and feel its power. (3) Place a meaningful reward (health upgrade, lore revelation, shortcut) at the top of the first wall-jump sequence, immediately reinforcing that new abilities lead to tangible gains. (4) After this initial playground, let the player backtrack to earlier areas where they noticed inaccessible walls, providing a cascade of 'I can go there now' realizations.

rubric.key: Explains ability-based gating in metroidvanias with the concept of visible but inaccessible goals
Describes at least two specific design decisions for the wall-jump acquisition moment
Connects the design to player empowerment with reasoning about why each choice amplifies the feeling

questions.question: A competitive card game has 5 character classes. Playtest data shows that one class (Rogue) wins 65% of matches while the weakest class (Healer) wins only 12%. Using balance concepts from the course, diagnose a likely structural cause and propose a specific mechanical fix that addresses the root cause without removing either class from the game.
questions.answer: A 65% win rate for Rogue likely indicates a dominant strategy: Rogue's mechanics allow it to win most matchups regardless of opponent class, suggesting the game's intransitive balance is broken. In a well-balanced 5-class system, each class should have favorable and unfavorable matchups, with no class dominating more than 2 others. Likely structural cause: Rogue probably has a mechanic that bypasses the counter-play systems other classes depend on (e.g., high burst damage that kills before Healer can heal, or evasion that negates the strengths of classes designed to counter it). Mechanical fix: Introduce or strengthen Healer's counter-play against Rogue specifically. For example, give Healer a 'purify' ability that removes Rogue's stealth/evasion buffs, creating a direct intransitive relationship (Healer counters Rogue). This preserves Rogue's identity and power against other matchups while ensuring it has at least one clear weakness, restoring the circular balance the game needs.

rubric.key: Correctly diagnoses the problem using balance concepts (dominant strategy, broken intransitive balance)
Identifies a plausible structural root cause rather than just noting the symptom
Proposes a specific mechanical fix that targets the root cause and preserves both classes

questions.question: Name two accessibility features from different disability categories (visual, auditory, motor, cognitive) and explain the 'curb-cut effect' for each: how the accessibility feature benefits players who do not have the disability it was designed for.
questions.answer: Feature 1 (Auditory): Subtitles with speaker identification. Designed for deaf and hard-of-hearing players. Curb-cut effect: Benefits all players in noisy environments (public transit, shared living spaces), players who speak a different native language and use subtitles for comprehension support, and players who play with sound off by choice. Feature 2 (Motor): Remappable controls. Designed for players with motor disabilities who cannot use standard button layouts. Curb-cut effect: Benefits left-handed players, players who prefer non-standard layouts, players switching between different controller types, and players with temporary injuries (broken finger, repetitive strain) who need to avoid specific buttons.

rubric.key: Two features from genuinely different disability categories, correctly categorized
Accurately describes the intended disability audience for each feature
Provides specific, plausible curb-cut scenarios for non-disabled players for each feature

questions.question: Describe the key phases of a game development pipeline from concept to launch. For each phase, name one deliverable that the game design team is primarily responsible for producing.
questions.answer: Concept Phase: The design team produces the one-page game concept document (pitch) that defines genre, core mechanic, unique hook, target audience, and aesthetic goals. Pre-Production Phase: The design team produces the game design document (GDD) detailing all mechanics, systems, content plans, and level designs in enough detail for implementation. Production (Alpha): The design team produces playtest reports and balance data from ongoing testing, driving iteration on implemented features and identifying design problems early. Production (Beta): The design team produces the final content matrix and balance spreadsheets, confirming all content is in place and numerical values are tuned. Launch and Live Operations: The design team produces update design documents for patches, balance adjustments, and new content based on live player data and community feedback.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies at least four major pipeline phases in the right order
Each deliverable is plausible and genuinely the design team's responsibility
Demonstrates understanding that design work is continuous throughout the pipeline, not front-loaded

major_assignments.title: Paper Prototype and Playtest Report
major_assignments.description: Design an original game mechanic, build a paper prototype, conduct at least four structured playtest sessions with different groups of testers, iterate on the design between sessions, and submit a comprehensive report documenting the entire process from initial concept through final iteration.
objectives: Demonstrate the ability to isolate and prototype a core game mechanic
Conduct structured playtests with neutral facilitation and systematic data collection
Show evidence of meaningful iteration driven by playtest findings across multiple cycles
Communicate design intent, process, and findings clearly in professional written form
deliverables: Physical paper prototype or detailed photographs of all prototype components at each iteration stage
Playtest observation forms from at least four sessions (minimum 2 testers per session)
Written report (6-8 pages) documenting original design intent, playtest methodology, findings from each session, changes made between sessions, and final reflection
One-page comparison document showing the initial design versus the final version with annotated changes and the evidence that motivated each change

criteria: Core mechanic is clearly identifiable, isolated, and testable
Prototype components are complete, functional, and playable without designer intervention
Rules are unambiguous and documented clearly enough for strangers to play
Design demonstrates creative thinking and originality
At least four sessions conducted with different tester groups
Observation forms capture both observed behavior and verbal feedback systematically
Facilitation was neutral without leading testers or defending the design
Both quantitative data and qualitative observations collected
Clear, specific changes made between playtest sessions
Every change is justified by specific playtest findings with evidence cited
At least three meaningful iterations documented with before/after descriptions
Final version shows measurable improvement over initial version
Professional writing quality with clear structure
Design goals and rationale articulated at the outset
Honest reflection on what worked and what did not
Proper report structure with introduction, methods, findings, iterations, and conclusion
Original and engaging core mechanic that is genuinely fun to interact with
Rigorous, well-documented playtest process with neutral facilitation
Evidence of meaningful, data-driven iteration across multiple cycles
Professional-quality written report with honest, reflective analysis

major_assignments.title: Game Analysis Essay: MDA and Player Psychology
major_assignments.description: Select a published game and write a comprehensive analytical essay applying the MDA framework and player psychology concepts from the course. The essay must identify the game's core mechanic, map its feedback loops, analyze which of the eight aesthetics it targets and how successfully, and evaluate how it addresses player motivation using Self-Determination Theory.
objectives: Apply the MDA framework to a complete game analysis with specificity and depth
Connect mechanical design decisions to their psychological effects on player motivation
Demonstrate critical thinking about why specific design choices succeed or fail
Write a well-structured analytical essay at college level
deliverables: Analytical essay (5-7 pages, double-spaced) with proper citations
MDA breakdown diagram showing mechanics, dynamics, and aesthetics with connecting arrows
Feedback loop diagram identifying at least two loops (positive or negative) in the game's systems

criteria: Core mechanic correctly identified and clearly distinguished from secondary mechanics
MDA analysis is thorough with specific examples from gameplay, not vague generalizations
Feedback loops correctly identified and diagrammed with accurate positive/negative labels
Connection between mechanics and aesthetics is traced through dynamics, not assumed directly
Player motivation analysis uses SDT framework correctly (autonomy, competence, relatedness)
Flow analysis identifies where the game succeeds and fails at maintaining the flow channel
Bartle player type analysis identifies which types the game serves well and which it neglects
Extrinsic vs. intrinsic motivation balance is evaluated with specific examples
Arguments are supported by specific evidence from gameplay, not assertions
Identifies at least one design decision that fails and explains why with MDA reasoning
Considers the game from multiple player type perspectives
Demonstrates original analytical thinking beyond surface observations
Clear thesis statement and logical essay structure
Professional academic writing quality
Proper citations of course readings and external sources
Diagrams are clear, labeled, and integrated into the text
Thorough and insightful MDA analysis with specific gameplay evidence
Strong integration of player psychology frameworks
Original critical thinking about design successes and failures
Professional academic writing with clear structure and proper citations

major_assignments.title: Complete Game Design Document
major_assignments.description: Create a comprehensive game design document for an original game concept that incorporates all frameworks, principles, and skills covered throughout the course. The GDD must include a one-page overview, detailed mechanic specifications, level design layouts, narrative outline, balance considerations, accessibility plan, and a playable prototype (paper or digital) demonstrating the core mechanic.
objectives: Synthesize all course concepts into a cohesive, original game design
Produce a professional-quality GDD that could guide actual development
Demonstrate the ability to think systematically about interconnected design problems
Create a playable prototype that validates the core mechanic described in the document
deliverables: One-page game overview (pitch document) with genre, platform, target audience, core mechanic, USP, and comparable titles

Full game design document (15-20 pages) covering mechanics (with input/rule/output specifications), level designs (at least 3 levels with annotated layouts), narrative outline, art direction references, balance approach, and accessibility plan
Content matrix spreadsheet listing all planned enemies, items, abilities, or equivalent game objects with their parameters
Playable prototype (paper or digital) demonstrating the core mechanic with playtest results from at least two external sessions
10-minute presentation with visual aids for the final class session

criteria: Core mechanic is original, clearly specified, and validated through prototyping
All mechanics documented in input/rule/output format with edge cases addressed
Level designs demonstrate conveyance, pacing, and the introduce/develop/twist pattern
Balance approach uses concepts from the course (transitive/intransitive relationships, cost curves, feedback loop management)
Narrative approach demonstrates understanding of embedded vs. emergent storytelling
Accessibility plan addresses at least three of the four disability categories with specific features
Art direction section provides enough visual reference for an artist to begin work
Content matrix is complete and internally consistent
Prototype is playable and meaningfully tests the core mechanic described in the GDD
Playtest results are documented and show evidence of iteration
The GDD reflects changes made based on prototype testing
Presentation is clear, well-structured, and delivered within time limit
Document is well-organized with consistent formatting and clear section hierarchy
Writing is precise enough for a developer to implement from the document alone
Personal design philosophy is evident throughout the choices made
Scope is realistic for the stated team size and timeline
Original, well-specified game concept with validated core mechanic
Comprehensive GDD covering all required sections at professional quality
Playable prototype with documented testing and iteration
Polished presentation demonstrating communication skills

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 5, 8, 11, 14) assessing theoretical knowledge of design frameworks, player psychology, prototyping methods, level design, narrative, balance, accessibility, and industry knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Weekly design journal entries, in-class exercises, and participation in playtest sessions and critique discussions

major_assignments.description: Paper Prototype and Playtest Report (150 pts), Game Analysis Essay (100 pts), and Complete Game Design Document (200 pts)

midterm_examination.description: Take-home midterm covering weeks 1-8: game definitions, MDA framework, player psychology, mechanics and dynamics, genre analysis, prototyping, and playtesting

final_project.description: Final game design document with playable prototype and in-class presentation demonstrating synthesis of all course concepts

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. In-class exercises, playtesting, and critique sessions cannot be made up. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 10% per day. Extensions may be granted if requested at least 48 hours before the deadline with a valid reason. The final GDD presentation cannot be rescheduled.
course_policies.collaboration: Playtesting naturally involves collaboration and is encouraged. For written assignments, the game analysis essay, and the final GDD, all work must be your own. Cite any external sources including published games referenced in your analysis.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Submitting another person's design as your own, fabricating playtest data, or plagiarizing written content will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: No specialized software is required. Bring a notebook or laptop for your design journal and basic prototyping supplies (index cards, dice, markers) for workshop sessions. A shared document platform (Google Docs) is used for collaborative exercises.
